stem: A set \( S \) has [[n]] elements. How many subsets does \( S \) have, including the empty set and \( S \) itself?
solution: Each element is independently either in or out of a subset, giving \( 2 \) choices per element, so \( S \) has \( 2^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \) subsets.
distractor_rationales: This squares the number of elements instead of raising 2 to that power.
This doubles the number of elements instead of using an exponent.
This excludes the empty set, counting only nonempty subsets.
This excludes both the empty set and \( S \) itself (proper nonempty subsets).
This is the number of subsets of a set with one fewer element.
This counts the orderings (permutations) of the elements, not the subsets.

stem: A set \( S \) has [[n]] elements. How many subsets of \( S \) contain exactly [[k]] elements?
solution: Choosing which [[k]] of the [[n]] elements belong to the subset is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts ordered selections (permutations) instead of unordered subsets.
This is the number of subsets of a set with only \( k \) elements, not a selection from \( n \).
This multiplies the set size by the subset size instead of using a combination.
This shifts both parameters down by one (a Pascal's-rule slip).
This is off by one in the size parameter.
This is off by one in the size parameter.

stem: A set \( S \) has [[n]] elements. How many subsets of \( S \) have at most [[k]] elements (including the empty set)?
solution: Sum the number of subsets of each size from \( 0 \) to \( [[k]] \): \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[k]]} \binom{[[n]]}{i} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts only subsets of size exactly \( k \), forgetting to accumulate the smaller sizes.
This confuses the cumulative count with a power-set-style count using \( k \) as the set size.
This is the total number of nonempty subsets of the whole set, ignoring the size cap.
This is off by one in the size cap.
This is the total number of subsets of the whole set, ignoring the size cap entirely.
This is off by one in the size cap.

stem: A set \( S \) has [[n]] elements. How many subsets of \( S \) are neither empty nor equal to \( S \) itself (proper, nonempty subsets)?
solution: Start from all \( 2^{[[n]]} \) subsets and remove the two excluded ones (the empty set and \( S \) itself): \( 2^{[[n]]} - 2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This excludes only the empty set, forgetting to also exclude \( S \) itself.
This excludes neither the empty set nor \( S \) itself.
This is the count for a set with one fewer element, not the proper-subset correction.
This wrongly halves the count before subtracting.
This subtracts one too many.
This subtracts two too many.

stem: Set \( A \) has [[m]] elements and set \( B \) has [[n]] elements. How many subsets does the Cartesian product \( A \times B \) have, including the empty set?
solution: \( A \times B \) has \( [[m]]\cdot[[n]] \) ordered pairs, so its power set has \( 2^{[[m]]\cdot[[n]]} = [[answer]] \) elements.
distractor_rationales: This adds the two set sizes in the exponent instead of multiplying them.
This squares the product-set size instead of using it as an exponent of 2.
This adds the two power-set sizes instead of using the product-set size as the exponent.
This is off by a factor of 2 (one fewer power of 2).
This excludes the empty set.
This counts orderings of the pairs rather than subsets.

stem: A set \( S \) has [[n]] elements, including a particular element \( x \). How many subsets of \( S \) contain \( x \)?
solution: Element \( x \) is fixed to be included; each of the other \( [[n]]-1 \) elements is independently in or out, giving \( 2^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \) subsets.
distractor_rationales: This is the total number of subsets of \( S \), forgetting that \( x \) must be included.
This subtracts one extra, excluding the empty remainder case.
This treats two elements as fixed instead of just \( x \).
This subtracts 2 from the full power set instead of halving it.
This doubles the remaining element count instead of using it as an exponent.
This gives the remaining element count itself, not the number of subsets it produces.

stem: A set \( S \) has [[n]] elements. How many ordered pairs of subsets \( (A, B) \) of \( S \) satisfy \( A \cap B = \varnothing \) (elements of \( S \) may lie in \( A \) only, in \( B \) only, or in neither)?
solution: Each element independently falls into exactly one of 3 categories (A only, B only, or neither), giving \( 3^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \) disjoint pairs.
distractor_rationales: This uses only 2 categories per element instead of 3.
This double-counts by treating each of \( A \) and \( B \) as an independent power set (allowing overlap).
This uses 4 categories per element, as if an element could be in both \( A \) and \( B \) too.
This subtracts one as if a case must be excluded.
This is the count for a set with one fewer element.
This multiplies instead of exponentiating.
